options.explanation: HF is an acid. In reaction with NaOH, it will produce NaF and H2O. NaF being a basic salt will produce a Ph greater than 7 at the equivalence point. Hence, this option is incorrect.
HCl is acid and will produce NaCl a neutral salt and H2O in reaction with NaOH. So, at the equilibrium point, the Ph will be 7 because both products formed are neutral.
LiOH is a base and will have a Ph greater than 7 and not 3.5. Hence, this option is incorrect.
NH3 is a base and will have a Ph greater than 7 and not 3.5. Hence, this option is incorrect.
H2SO4 is an acid. In reaction with NaOH, it will produce NaHSO4 and H2O. NaHSO4 being an acidic salt will produce a Ph smaller than 7 at the equivalence point. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: HCl is acid and will produce NaCl a neutral salt and H2O in reaction with NaOH. So, at the equilibrium point, the Ph will be 7 because both products formed are neutral.

questions.text: Which of the following will occur if a quantity of acetic acid is added to a solution of potassium hydroxide at pH 11?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The number of free protons per liter of solution will increase.
options.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid, so it will donate its protons to the potassium hydroxide, thereby decreasing the number of free protons in the solution. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Titration will tend to neutralize the solution.
options.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid, while potassium hydroxide is a strong base. When they are mixed, they will react to form water and potassium acetate, a salt. This reaction will consume both the acid and the base, thereby neutralizing the solution.

options.text: The acetic acid will act as a weak base
options.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid, not a weak base. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Acetate ion will precipitate out of solution.
options.explanation: Acetate ions are soluble and will not precipitate. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Potassium hydroxide has a pH of 11 and is basic. When acid is added, it will undergo a neutralization reaction, leading to a change in pH. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When acetic acid, a weak acid, is added to a solution of potassium hydroxide, a strong base, they will react to form water and potassium acetate, neutralizing the solution. This reaction consumes both the acid and the base, resulting in a neutralized solution. The other options are incorrect because acetic acid is a weak acid, not a weak base, acetate ions do not precipitate, and the addition of acid to a basic solution will change the pH, not keep it constant.

questions.text: Which of the following is always amphoteric in nature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nucleic acids are not amphoteric. They contain a phosphate group which makes them acidic. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Proteins are made up of amino acids which have an acidic group (-COOH) and a basic group (-NH2). So, they have a free carboxylic (-COOH-) and a free amino group (-NH2+) at each end which can react with both acids and bases. So, they are amphoteric.
Carbohydrates are not amphoteric. They are made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, and they do not contain any acidic or basic functional groups. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Lipids are not amphoteric. They are organic molecules that are made up of mostly carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. They do not contain any acidic or basic functional groups. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Electrolytes are not always amphoteric. Some electrolytes are acids or bases while some may be amphoteric. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Proteins are considered amphoteric because they contain both acidic and basic functional groups. The presence of carboxylic (-COOH) and amino (-NH2) groups in amino acids allows proteins to react with both acids and bases, exhibiting amphoteric behavior. Nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids, and electrolytes do not possess the necessary acidic and basic groups to be considered amphoteric.

questions.text: In the titration setup, if you introduce 15 mL of the NaOH with an unknown molarity into the flask and then add 5 drops of phenolphthalein indicator, what will you observe?

options.text: A pinkish color will appear throughout the solution.

options.text: A blue color will appear throughout the solution.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because phenolphthalein does not exhibit a blue color in any pH range; it remains colorless or turns pink in basic solutions.

options.text: There will be a temporary pinkish color that will dissipate.

options.explanation: This is incorrect because phenolphthalein does not turn blue at any point; therefore, this scenario cannot occur.

questions.explanation: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is a strong base. Phenolphthalein. indicator is colorless in acidic/neutral solutions but turns a distinct pink color in basic solutions (pH > 8.2). Therefore, adding it to NaOH will result in a pink solution.

questions.text: Human blood is slightly alkaline with pH 7.4 and it does not change significantly with the addition of a small amount of an acid or a base because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Serum proteins present in the blood act as buffer
options.explanation: Human blood is slightly alkaline with a pH of 7.4. The pH of blood is maintained within a narrow range, and it does not change significantly by the addition of a small amount of acid or base because of the presence of serum proteins in blood. Serum proteins acts as buffer that help to maintain the pH of blood within a narrow range. They can accept or donate hydrogen ions (H+) to resist changes in pH when an acid or base is added to the blood.

options.text: Na-K channels maintain the flow of hydrogen ions
options.explanation: Na-K channels are involved in the transport of sodium and potassium ions across cell membranes and do not play a role in maintaining the pH of blood.

options.text: Hb acts as a buffer and also helps in its coagulation
options.explanation: Hemoglobin in blood is involved in oxygen transport and does not play a role in maintaining the pH of blood.

Human blood is not neutral. Blood pH is slightly basic due to presence of bicarbonate (weak base) in it. The blood pH in our bodies lies between 7.35 to 7.45, which means blood is slightly alkaline in nature.

questions.explanation: Human blood contains several buffer systems to resist pH changes. The primary buffer system in the blood plasma (serum) is the carbonic acid-bicarbonate system (H2CO3/HCO3−), along with phosphate buffers and serum proteins like albumin.

questions.text: H3PO4 is dibasic in nature and forms a buffer against:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: H3PO4 is a dibasic acid, meaning it has two acidic hydrogen atoms that can dissociate in water to form two H+ ions. It forms a buffer solution with its conjugate base, H2PO4-, which can resist changes in pH. When mixed with a strong base like NaOH, the buffer neutralizes the added OH- ions, maintaining the pH.
Strong acids would further lower the pH, rather than form a stable buffer with H3PO4.
While a weak base may interact with H3PO4, it does not provide the same buffering capacity against strong bases as the conjugate base does.
H3PO4 primarily forms a buffer with strong bases by neutralizing them, not simultaneously with both strong and weak bases.

questions.explanation: H3PO4 is a dibasic acid, meaning it can donate two protons (H+ ions). When it forms a buffer with its conjugate base, H2PO4-, it can effectively resist changes in pH when a strong base like NaOH is added. The strong base reacts with the weak acid (H2PO4-) to form its conjugate base (PO43-), neutralizing the added OH- ions and maintaining a stable pH. This is why Option A is correct. Option B is incorrect because a strong acid would not create a buffering effect with H3PO4. Option C is incorrect because a weak base does not provide the buffering interaction needed as effectively as a strong base does. Option D is incorrect because H3PO4 cannot buffer simultaneously against both strong and weak bases; its primary buffering action is against strong bases.

questions.text: Which one of the following can be used as an acid-base indicator by a visually impaired student?

options.explanation: Litmus paper is a commonly used acid-base indicator that changes color when exposed to acids or bases. However, it primarily relies on visual cues, which may not be suitable for visually impaired students.

Turmeric is known to change color from yellow to red in the presence of bases, making it a natural indicator. However, it might not be the most practical choice for a visually impaired student due to its reliance on visual changes in color.

Vanilla essence has a characteristic smell that can be influenced by changes in pH. It can be a suitable choice for a visually impaired student as they can detect the changes in smell. Therefore, it can effectively serve as an acid-base indicator in this context.

Petunia leaves contain pigments that can change color in response to changes in pH. However, since it primarily depends on visual changes, it may not be the most suitable option for visually impaired students.

questions.explanation: The question is asking which one of the given substances can be utilized as an acid-base indicator by a visually impaired student. An acid-base indicator is a substance that undergoes distinct observable changes in color or odor when exposed to acidic or basic solutions, allowing the user to determine the pH of a solution based on the changes.Vanilla essence has a distinct smell that can be used as an indicator. It has a characteristic fragrance that changes depending on the pH of the solution it is added to. This makes it a suitable choice for a visually impaired student to distinguish between acidic and basic solutions based on the change in smell.

options.text: After washing with plenty of water, apply solution of sodium hydroxide on the hand

options.text: Neutralise the acid with a strong alkali
options.explanation: This option is not specific about the type of strong alkali to be used or the method of application. Without clear instructions, this option is not as effective as option b, which specifies the use of sodium hydrogen carbonate paste for neutralization.

The application of a paste of sodium hydrogen carbonate (baking soda) helps to neutralize the acid, thereby reducing the damage caused by the acid.Options a and c do not specifically address the neutralization of the acid, which is crucial in this scenario. questions.text: To prevent tooth decay, we are advised to brush our teeth regularly. The nature of the toothpaste commonly used is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Toothpaste is not typically acidic. The use of an acidic toothpaste could potentially damage the tooth enamel and contribute to tooth decay, which is contrary to the intended purpose of using toothpaste.

While toothpaste can have a neutral pH, it is not commonly described as neutral. The neutral pH may not effectively aid in the prevention of tooth decay or maintenance of oral hygiene, as it might not effectively combat the acids produced by bacteria in the mouth.

The most common toothpaste is slightly basic, or alkaline in nature. Basic toothpaste helps neutralize the acids produced by bacteria in the mouth, maintaining a balanced pH level and preventing tooth decay. This option aligns with the standard formulation of toothpaste used for maintaining dental hygiene.

Toothpaste is not corrosive. If it were corrosive, it would cause harm to the tooth enamel and the soft tissues in the mouth, leading to various dental issues and complications. Therefore, this option is not accurate in describing the nature of toothpaste commonly used.

questions.explanation: Toothpaste typically contains mild abrasives, fluorides, detergents, and other ingredients. The basic nature of toothpaste helps in neutralizing the acids produced by bacteria in the mouth, thereby helping to maintain the pH balance and prevent tooth decay. The basic components in toothpaste also aid in the removal of plaque and food particles, ensuring better oral hygiene.Options a, b, and d are not typically characteristic of toothpaste commonly used. Toothpaste is not generally acidic, neutral, or corrosive, as these properties would be harmful to the teeth and oral health. Therefore, option c, "Basic," is the most appropriate and accurate description of the nature of the toothpaste commonly used for maintaining dental hygiene and preventing tooth decay.

questions.text: Partial neutralisation of a polybasic acid gives
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An acid salt is formed when only a part of the replaceable hydrogen of a polybasic acid is replaced by a metal. This results in a salt that still retains acidic properties. The formation of an acid salt is a common occurrence in cases of partial neutralization of polybasic acids.

A basic salt is not typically formed during the partial neutralization of a polybasic acid. Basic salts usually arise from the reaction of a weak acid and a strong base, leading to a salt that has basic properties. Thus, option b is not an accurate description of the product resulting from partial neutralization of a polybasic acid.

A normal salt is formed through the complete neutralization of an acid with a base. It does not specifically apply to the partial neutralization of a polybasic acid, so it is not an appropriate option in this context.

A double salt refers to a combination of two distinct salts that are combined and crystallized together. It is not typically related to the process of partial neutralization of a polybasic acid, making option d an inaccurate choice.

questions.explanation: When a polybasic acid is not fully neutralized by a base, the resulting product is an acid salt. This type of salt still retains acidic properties and may be partially ionized in water. Its pH will be less than 7, indicating acidity. The formation of an acid salt occurs when only a portion of the acidic hydrogen atoms in the polybasic acid are replaced by metal or other cations.

questions.text: How many times stronger is an acid with a pH of 2 than an acid with a pH of 5?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: A pH of 2 is three times as strong.
options.explanation: This option does not accurately represent the relationship between the pH values. As the pH scale is logarithmic, the difference of three pH units implies a much greater difference in strength than a simple threefold increase.

options.text: A pH of 2 is one thousand times as strong.
options.explanation: This option accurately represents the relationship between the pH values. As each unit change on the pH scale represents a tenfold difference in acidity, the difference of three pH units (from 2 to 5) represents a 10^3 or 1,000-fold difference in strength, making option B the correct choice.

options.text: A pH of 2 is three times as weak.
options.explanation: This option does not accurately represent the concept of pH and the relationship between acid strength and pH. It suggests that a higher pH value implies greater acidity, which is not the case.

options.text: A pH of 2 is one thousand times as weak.
options.explanation: Similar to option C, this option does not accurately represent the concept of pH. A lower pH value indicates greater acidity, not weakness, so option D is not a valid choice.

questions.explanation: The pH scale is logarithmic, meaning that each whole pH value below 7 is ten times more acidic than the next higher value. Therefore, a pH of 2 is ten times more acidic than a pH of 3, and 100 times more acidic than a pH of 4, and 1,000 times more acidic than a pH of 5. Thus, an acid with a pH of 2 is 1,000 times stronger than an acid with a pH of 5.Among the options provided, the correct choice is:B. A pH of 2 is one thousand times as strong.

questions.text: An aqueous solution of which of the following compounds will act as the best conductor of electricity?

questions.explanation: The question asks which of the given compounds, when dissolved in water, will act as the best conductor of electricity in the resulting aqueous solution. questions.text: Which of the following acts as a buffer
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: HNO2 (nitrous acid) is a weak acid, and NaNO2 (sodium nitrite) is its conjugate base. This combination forms a buffer solution because it can maintain the pH level upon the addition of small amounts of acid or base.
HCl is a strong acid, and KCl is a neutral salt. This combination does not form a buffer solution because it lacks a weak component to provide resistance to pH changes.
HNO3 is a strong acid, and NH4NO3 is a salt of a weak base with a strong acid. This pair cannot act as a buffer because it does not contain a weak acid or its conjugate base.
NaOH is a strong base, and NaCl is a neutral salt. This combination does not form a buffer solution because it lacks a weak acid or base and its conjugate.

questions.explanation: A buffer solution is typically composed of a weak acid and its conjugate base, or a weak base and its conjugate acid. This combination allows the solution to resist changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added. In this case, Option A (HNO2 and NaNO2) forms a buffer because HNO2 is a weak acid and NaNO2 is its conjugate base. This pairing can effectively stabilize the pH of a solution. In contrast, Option B (HCl and KCl) and Option C (HNO3 and NH4NO3) both include strong acids, which do not contribute to buffering capacity. Option D (NaOH and NaCl) involves a strong base and a neutral salt, which also cannot form a buffer solution.

questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest conjugate base?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chloride ion is the conjugate base of the strong acid, HCl. While it is stable, it is not as strong a conjugate base as some other options.
Sulfate ion is a conjugate base of a strong acid. While it is stable, it is not as strong a conjugate base as some other options.
Acetate ion is the conjugate base of acetic acid (CH3COOH), a weak acid. It can stabilize the negative charge through resonance, making it the strongest conjugate base among the options provided.
Nitrate ion is the conjugate base of nitric acid, which is a strong acid. Similar to sulfate ion, it is stable but not as strong a conjugate base as acetate ion.

questions.explanation: In the context of acids and bases, the strength of a conjugate base is determined by its ability to accept a proton. A stronger conjugate base is better able to stabilize the negative charge that results after accepting a proton.

questions.text: Which of the following is an amphoteric (can accept and give protons) ions
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the metaphosphate ion, known for its acidic behavior, but it does not exhibit amphoteric properties as it does not readily accept a proton.
This is the hypophosphite ion, which primarily acts as a reducing agent and lacks the ability to show amphoteric behavior by accepting a proton.
This is phosphoric acid, a neutral molecule that functions as an acid rather than an ion, and does not exhibit amphoteric properties.
The dihydrogen phosphate ion, H2PO4-, can donate a proton to become HPO42- or accept a proton to form H3PO4, demonstrating its ability to act as both an acid and a base.

questions.explanation: An amphoteric species, such as H2PO4-, can act as both an acid and a base. H2PO4- can donate a proton to form HPO42-, acting as an acid, or it can accept a proton to become H3PO4, acting as a base. This dual capability is what makes it amphoteric. The other options do not exhibit this dual proton exchange ability: HPO3- and H2PO2- do not typically accept protons, and H3PO4 is a neutral molecule, not an ion, and functions primarily as an acid.

HCOOH ⇌ HCOO- + H+Given that the Ka values are:- Ka(CH3COOH) = 10-5- Ka(HCOOH) = 10-4The dissociation constant (Ka) is the ratio of the concentration of the products to the concentration of the reactants.For acetic acid (CH3COOH), if x is the concentration of the acid that dissociates, then the concentration of H+ is √(10-5 * x).For formic acid (HCOOH), if y is the concentration of the acid that dissociates, then the concentration of H+ is √(10-4 * y).To find the concentration of CH3COOH that will have the same [H+] as obtained from 10-2 M HCOOH, we set √(10-5 * x) equal to √(10-4 * 10-2) and solve for x.√(10-5 * x) = √(10-6)√x = 10-3x = (10-3)2x = 10-6x = 10-1Therefore, the concentration of CH3COOH that will have the same [H+] as obtained from 10-2 M HCOOH is 10-1 M.

Sodium nitrate is derived from a strong base (NaOH) and a strong acid (HNO3). As both parts are strong, it results in a neutral salt.
Potassium chloride originates from a strong base (KOH) and a strong acid (HCl), resulting in a neutral salt solution.
Ammonium chloride is formed from a weak base (NH3) and a strong acid (HCl), making it an acidic salt.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Na2CO3 because it is a basic salt. This type of salt is formed from the reaction of a strong base, such as NaOH, with a weak acid, like H2CO3. questions.text: Which of the following is not an amphiprotic species?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The bicarbonate ion (HCO3–) can donate a proton to become CO32– or accept a proton to become H2CO3, acting as both an acid and a base, making it amphiprotic.
The hydrogen phosphate ion (HPO42–) can donate a proton to become PO43– or accept a proton to become H2PO4–, allowing it to function as both an acid and a base. Thus, it is amphiprotic.
The hydroxide ion (OH–) can only accept a proton to become water (H2O), and cannot donate a proton. Therefore, it only acts as a base and is not amphiprotic.
The dihydrogen phosphate ion (H2PO4–) can donate a proton to become HPO42– or accept a proton to form H3PO4. This dual ability makes it amphiprotic.

questions.explanation: An amphiprotic species is one that can both donate and accept protons, allowing it to act as both an acid and a base. The bicarbonate ion (HCO3–), hydrogen phosphate ion (HPO42–), and dihydrogen phosphate ion (H2PO4–) all have this capability, making them amphiprotic. However, the hydroxide ion (OH–) can only accept protons, as it does not have any protons to donate. Thus, it acts solely as a base and is not considered amphiprotic, making it the correct answer to the question.

questions.text: The pH value of a 0.01 M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. The pKa of the acid will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This value is significantly higher than the calculated pKa of 6.3
This value is significantly lower than the calculated pKa of 6.3.
This value is lower than the calculated pKa of 6.3 but not as low as option b
This is the right option.

questions.explanation: Certainly, to find the pKa of an acid from the given pH value, we first find the concentration of H+ ions using the formula pH = -log[H+]. From this, we get [H+] = antilog(-4.15) = 7.08 × 10-5 M.Since the concentration of the conjugate base [A-] is the same as the concentration of H+ in a 0.01 M solution, we have [A-] = 7.08 × 10-5 M.The concentration of undissociated acid, [HA], can be found by subtracting the concentration of the dissociated ions from the initial concentration of the acid, giving us [HA] = 0.01 - 7.08 × 10-5 = 0.009929 M.Using the expression for the acid dissociation constant Ka, we find that Ka = (7.08 × 10-5) * (7.08 × 10-5) / 0.009929 = 5.05 × 10-7.Taking the negative logarithm of Ka gives us the pKa value: pKa = -log(5.05 × 10-7) ≈ 6.3.

This is the correct answer. When a metal reacts with an acid, it typically undergoes a displacement reaction, producing both hydrogen gas and a salt. This can be represented by the general equation:Metal + Acid → Salt + Hydrogen gas

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the reaction of a metal with an acid results in the formation of both a salt and hydrogen gas. During this reaction, the metal displaces hydrogen ions from the acid, leading to the release of hydrogen gas. This type of reaction is called a displacement reaction. Therefore, option C is correct.Option A is incorrect because hydrogen gas is not the sole product; a salt also forms. Option B is incorrect as only salt is not the complete set of products. Option D is incorrect because the reaction does indeed produce both salt and hydrogen gas.

questions.text: Which of the following is acidic in nature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Apple juice is acidic due to the presence of organic acids such as citric acid and malic acid, which contribute to its tart taste.
Soap solutions are typically basic in nature because they contain alkaline substances that neutralize acids.
Slaked lime, also known as calcium hydroxide, is a basic compound and is not acidic. It is often used to neutralize acidic soils.
In this context, lime likely refers to quicklime or calcium oxide, both of which are basic substances, not acidic.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Apple juice. It is acidic because it contains organic acids such as citric acid and malic acid, which give it a characteristic tart flavor. In contrast, soap solution is basic due to its alkaline constituents, slaked lime is a base used to neutralize acids, and lime (calcium oxide) is a basic compound used in various industrial processes to neutralize acidic substances.

questions.text: Plaster of Paris hardens by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Plaster of Paris does not release carbon dioxide during the hardening process. Instead, it involves a different chemical interaction with water.
This is incorrect. The hardening process of plaster of Paris does not involve a transformation into calcium carbonate (CaCO3).

This is correct. Plaster of Paris hardens by reacting with water. The reaction involves hydration, where plaster of Paris (calcium sulfate hemihydrate) combines with water to form gypsum (calcium sulfate dihydrate), which hardens into a solid structure.
This is incorrect. Plaster of Paris absorbs water during the hardening process, rather than expelling it. The addition of water is essential for the formation of gypsum.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that plaster of Paris hardens by reacting with water. When water is added to plaster of Paris, it undergoes hydration, transforming into gypsum, which sets into a solid structure. The other options are incorrect because they describe processes that do not occur during the hardening of plaster of Paris. It does not release CO2 or transform into CaCO3, nor does it expel water during the process.

questions.text: Which of the following processes represents a neutralisation reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Vinegar, which is acetic acid, neutralises the alkaline nature of a wasp sting through an acid-base reaction.
Toothpaste contains basic compounds that neutralise the acidic environment in the mouth caused by bacterial metabolism.

options.text: Sodium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid to form sodium chloride
options.explanation: This is a classic example of a neutralisation reaction where a base (sodium hydroxide) reacts with an acid (hydrochloric acid) to produce water and a neutral salt (sodium chloride).
Each scenario represents a neutralisation reaction: vinegar neutralises wasp stings, toothpaste neutralises oral acids, and sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid form a neutral salt.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D: All of these. Each option demonstrates a neutralisation process where an acid reacts with a base to produce a neutral substance:Option A: Vinegar neutralises the wasp sting, which is basic in nature.Option B: Toothpaste neutralises acids in the mouth.Option C: Sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid react to form a neutral salt, sodium chloride, and water.Thus, all options illustrate neutralisation reactions.

questions.text: When the stopper of a bottle containing colourless liquid was removed, the bottle smelled like vinegar. The liquid in the bottle could be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hydrochloric acid typically emits a pungent and irritating odor, not similar to vinegar. Therefore, it is unlikely to be the liquid in the bottle.
Sodium hydroxide is a strong base with no vinegar-like smell. It usually feels soapy and has no distinct odor that matches vinegar.
Acetic acid is the primary component of vinegar, responsible for its distinctive smell. This matches the description of the liquid in the bottle.
Sodium hydrogen carbonate, also known as baking soda, is typically odorless and does not have a vinegar-like smell.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Acetic acid solution. Acetic acid is the main component of vinegar, which gives it the characteristic vinegar smell described in the question. Hydrochloric acid has a sharp and pungent odor, which is quite different from vinegar. Sodium hydroxide solution is a strong base and typically has no noticeable odor that resembles vinegar. Saturated sodium hydrogen carbonate solution is generally odorless or may have a slight alkaline smell but not like vinegar.

questions.text: Universal indicator solution is named as such because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Although the name suggests worldwide availability, the term 'universal' refers to its application across the entire pH scale, not its distribution.
This is incorrect. The universal indicator's appearance changes based on the pH of the solution it is in, showing a range of colors from red to violet.

options.text: It can be used for the entire pH range
options.explanation: This is correct. The universal indicator is designed to provide a color change over the entire pH spectrum, from very acidic to very basic solutions.
This is incorrect. Only the statement regarding its use over the entire pH range is correct. The other options are not accurate.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the universal indicator solution can be used for the entire pH range. This indicator is specifically formulated to exhibit a color change over the complete pH scale, from 0 to 14, indicating whether a solution is acidic, neutral, or basic. This characteristic makes it 'universal' in terms of application. The other options are incorrect: the term 'universal' does not refer to its availability, nor does it describe a singular appearance, as its color varies with pH.

questions.text: When few drops of lemon are mixed with milk,(i) it turns sour(ii) no change takes place(iii) properties of milk are changed(iv) properties of milk remain the same.Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. When lemon juice, which contains citric acid, is added to milk, it causes a reaction that changes the properties of the milk.
This statement is partially correct. While the milk does turn sour (option i), a change occurs (contradicting option ii), making this combination incorrect.
This is the correct statement. The addition of lemon makes the milk sour and alters its properties by causing the milk proteins to coagulate.
This statement is incomplete. Although the milk turns sour (option i), it also undergoes a change in its physical properties, which is not accounted for by this option.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is (i) and (iii). When lemon juice, which contains citric acid, is added to milk, it causes the milk proteins to coagulate, changing its properties and making it sour. Option (ii) is incorrect because a chemical reaction does occur, altering the milk. Option (iv) is incorrect as the properties of the milk do not remain the same.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a hydrated salt?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Blue vitriol, or copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), is a hydrated salt. The presence of water molecules in its formula confirms that it is hydrated.
Baking soda, known as sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), is not a hydrated salt as it lacks water molecules in its chemical structure, making it an anhydrous compound.
Washing soda, or sodium carbonate decahydrate (Na2CO3·10H2O), is a hydrated salt. Its formula clearly shows the presence of ten water molecules.
Epsom salt, also known as magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MgSO4·7H2O), is a hydrated salt with seven water molecules included in its chemical formula.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B – Baking soda, or sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), is not a hydrated salt because it does not include water molecules in its formula. In contrast, Blue vitriol (CuSO4·5H2O), Washing soda (Na2CO3·10H2O), and Epsom salt (MgSO4·7H2O) each contain water molecules, indicating they are hydrated salts.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are correct?I. The tangy taste of cola drinks is due to carbonic acid which is an organic acid.II. Inorganic acids are prepared artificially in the industries and are extremely corrosive.III. Hydrochloric acid reacts with metals to varying degrees therefore, it is stored in glass bottles.IV. Organic acids are weak acids and are used as food supplements.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As statement I and III are incorrect, this option is wrong.
As statement I is incorrect, this option is wrong.
As both statements II and IV are correct, this option will be marked as the correct option.
As only option C is correct, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: I. The tangy taste of cola drinks is due to carbonic acid which is an organic acid:This statement is not correct. The tangy taste in cola drinks is mainly due to citric acid and phosphoric acid, which are not organic acids.II. Inorganic acids are prepared artificially in the industries and are extremely corrosive:This statement is correct. Inorganic acids, such as sulfuric acid and hydrochloric acid, are often prepared industrially and can be highly corrosive.III. Hydrochloric acid reacts with metals to varying degrees, therefore, it is stored in glass bottles:This statement is not entirely correct. Hydrochloric acid is indeed reactive with metals, but it is usually stored in plastic or other acid-resistant containers rather than glass.IV. Organic acids are weak acids and are used as food supplements:This statement is correct. Many organic acids, such as citric acid found in citrus fruits, are weak acids and can be used as food supplements.

questions.text: A compound is prepared from gypsum upon heating to a temperature of 373 K and it changes back to gypsum on adding water. Which is the incorrect statement about the compound?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The compound is used for setting fractured bones.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Plaster of Paris is commonly used in medical applications for setting fractured bones due to its ability to form a hard solid when mixed with water.

options.text: The compound is called Plaster of Paris which is calcium sulphate dihydrate with a formula CaSO4·2H2O.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Plaster of Paris is actually calcium sulfate hemihydrate, with the formula CaSO4·1/2H2O, not CaSO4·2H2O. It is called hemihydrate because it contains half a water molecule per calcium sulfate unit.

options.text: If heated at a higher temperature, the compound becomes dehydrated and is called dead burnt plaster.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. When Plaster of Paris is heated to a higher temperature, it loses water content and becomes dead burnt plaster, which is more stable and does not set with water.
This option is incorrect because while statement A is correct, statement B is inaccurate. Plaster of Paris does not have the formula CaSO4·2H2O.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. Plaster of Paris is not calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO4·2H2O); it is calcium sulfate hemihydrate (CaSO4·1/2H2O). Option A is correct because Plaster of Paris is used in medical applications for setting bones. Option C is also correct as heating Plaster of Paris further results in dead burnt plaster. Option D is incorrect because it combines one correct and one incorrect statement, which makes the whole option incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding bases is false?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Bases produce hydroxide ions when dissolved in water
options.explanation: This statement is true. When bases are dissolved in water, they dissociate to release hydroxide ions (OH-), which is a characteristic behavior of bases.
This statement is true. Bases often feel slippery or soapy because they can react with the natural oils and fats on the skin, forming a soap-like substance.

options.text: Bases are extremely corrosive in nature
options.explanation: This statement is partially true. While many strong bases can be corrosive, the level of corrosiveness varies depending on the concentration and specific base in question.

options.text: Basic solutions are non-conducting in nature
options.explanation: This statement is false. Basic solutions conduct electricity due to the presence of ions, such as hydroxide ions (OH-), which facilitate the flow of electric current.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Basic solutions are non-conducting in nature. This statement is false because basic solutions can conduct electricity. The presence of ions, particularly hydroxide ions (OH-), allows them to conduct electric current. In contrast, the other statements are true: bases produce hydroxide ions in water (Option A), feel slippery or soapy (Option B), and can be corrosive depending on their concentration and type (Option C).

questions.text: Choose the incorrect statement among the following.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Tamarind, tomato, and sour milk are the natural sources of tartaric acid, citric acid, and lactic acid respectively.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. While tamarind is a source of tartaric acid and sour milk contains lactic acid, citric acid is not found in tomatoes but in citrus fruits like lemons and oranges.

options.text: The atmosphere of Venus is made up of thick white and yellowish clouds of sulfuric acid.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Venus's atmosphere is indeed dominated by thick clouds primarily composed of sulfuric acid.

options.text: Bleaching powder is used for disinfecting drinking water.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Bleaching powder is utilized for disinfecting drinking water due to its chlorine content, which effectively kills bacteria and other microorganisms.

options.text: During electrolysis of brine, Cl2 gas is given off at the anode and H2 gas at the cathode.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In the electrolysis of brine, chlorine gas evolves at the anode, and hydrogen gas is released at the cathode.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A. This statement is incorrect because, although tamarind is a source of tartaric acid and sour milk contains lactic acid, citric acid is not found in tomatoes. Instead, it is found in citrus fruits such as lemons and oranges. Option B is correct as Venus's atmosphere is composed of sulfuric acid clouds. Option C is correct because bleaching powder is indeed used to disinfect drinking water. Option D is correct since, during the electrolysis of brine, chlorine gas is released at the anode and hydrogen at the cathode.

questions.text: Baking powder is a mixture of X and a mild edible acid such as Y. When it is heated, Z is produced which makes bread and cake soft and spongy. X, Y and Z are respectively
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Sodium hydrogen carbonate, tartaric acid, CO2
options.explanation: This option is correct. Baking powder is a mixture of sodium hydrogen carbonate (X) and a mild edible acid like tartaric acid (Y). When it is heated, it releases carbon dioxide (CO2) (Z), which is responsible for making bread and cakes soft and spongy.
This option is incorrect. While carbon dioxide (CO2) is produced, baking powder contains sodium hydrogen carbonate, not sodium carbonate, and a different acid, such as tartaric acid, not acetic acid.
This option is incorrect. Baking powder does not contain sodium hydroxide, and it does not release hydrogen gas (H2) when heated; it releases carbon dioxide (CO2).
This option is incorrect. Baking powder does not contain sodium chloride, and it does not release hydrogen gas (H2); it releases carbon dioxide (CO2).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that baking powder is a mixture of sodium hydrogen carbonate and a mild edible acid such as tartaric acid. When baking powder is heated, it produces carbon dioxide (CO2), which causes the dough to rise and makes baked goods soft and spongy. Option A correctly identifies these components and the gas produced. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they either list the wrong components or the wrong gas produced. Baking powder must contain sodium hydrogen carbonate and a suitable acid to produce carbon dioxide when heated.

questions.text: Which of the following salts will undergo hydrolysis in water, resulting in a basic solution?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) undergoes hydrolysis, forming an acidic solution due to the presence of NH4+ which releases H+ ions.
Sodium chloride (NaCl) does not undergo hydrolysis because both Na+ and Cl- ions are spectator ions that do not affect the pH of the solution.
Potassium nitrate (KNO3) does not undergo hydrolysis as both K+ and NO3- are neutral ions that do not change the pH of the solution.
Sodium acetate (CH3COONa) undergoes hydrolysis, forming a basic solution. The acetate ion (CH3COO-) reacts with water to form OH- ions, increasing the pH.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Sodium acetate (CH3COONa) because it undergoes hydrolysis to form a basic solution. The acetate ion (CH3COO-) reacts with water to produce hydroxide ions (OH-), increasing the pH and resulting in a basic solution. On the other hand, ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) forms an acidic solution due to the formation of H+ ions. Sodium chloride (NaCl) and potassium nitrate (KNO3) do not undergo significant hydrolysis, as their constituent ions do not react with water to alter the pH.

questions.text: Inability of any solvent to differentiate among the relative strength of all acids is termed as
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Basicity refers to the ability of a compound to act as a base. It is not related to how a solvent interacts with acids.
Solubility is about how well a substance dissolves in a solvent, not about comparing the strength of acids.
Buffer action involves the ability of a solution to resist changes in pH, which is not about differentiating acid strengths.
The levelling effect is when a solvent cannot distinguish between the strengths of different acids because they all appear to be equally strong in that solvent.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is levelling effect. This phenomenon occurs when a solvent, typically a very strong base or acid, causes all acids (or bases) to appear to have the same strength because they are fully ionized in the solvent. This is not related to basicity, which is about the nature of a base, solubility, which concerns how substances dissolve, or buffer action, which deals with the capacity of a solution to resist pH changes.

questions.text: Which of the following elements forms an amphoteric as well as normal oxide?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc forms an amphoteric oxide, ZnO, which can react with both acids and bases.
Sulfur forms acidic oxides like SO2 and SO3, which do not exhibit amphoteric behavior.
Nitrogen oxides (e.g., NO, NO2) are typically acidic and do not exhibit amphoteric properties.
Water (H2O) is a neutral oxide, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has unique reactivity, but neither is amphoteric.

questions.explanation: Zinc is the correct answer because zinc oxide (ZnO) is amphoteric, meaning it can react with both acids and bases to form salts and water. In contrast, sulfur forms SO2 and SO3, which are acidic oxides; nitrogen forms oxides like NO and NO2, which are also acidic. Hydrogen forms water, which is neutral, and hydrogen peroxide, which has unique reactivity but is not amphoteric.

questions.text: If the pH of bleach is 12.6, then the concentration of hydrogen ions in this solution is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the concentration of hydrogen ions calculated using the formula [H+] = 10-pH. For pH 12.6, [H+] = 10-12.6.
This is an incorrect calculation of hydrogen ion concentration. It does not correspond to a pH of 12.6.
This option incorrectly represents the pH value as the concentration of hydrogen ions. pH is a logarithmic measure, not a direct concentration.
This value is excessively high and incorrect for a hydrogen ion concentration in a solution with pH 12.6.

questions.explanation: The pH of a solution is defined as the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration: pH = -log[H+]. Therefore, to find the concentration of hydrogen ions, we use the formula [H+] = 10-pH. For a pH of 12.6, the concentration of hydrogen ions is [H+] = 10-12.6, which equals approximately 2.51 × 10−13 mol/L. The other options either incorrectly interpret the pH as a concentration, or provide values that do not match the formula for calculating hydrogen ion concentration.

questions.text: A basic buffer is prepared by mixing a weak base and a salt of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Weak acid with a strong acid
options.explanation: This combination would not create a basic buffer. Instead, it would not effectively resist changes in pH.

options.text: Weak base with a strong acid
options.explanation: This combination forms a salt, which is part of a basic buffer system. The weak base provides the base component, and the salt provides the conjugate acid form.

options.text: Weak acid with a strong base
options.explanation: This combination would create an acidic buffer, not a basic one, as it involves a weak acid and its conjugate base.

options.text: Weak base with a strong base
options.explanation: This combination doesn't work as a buffer because it doesn't provide the necessary conjugate acid form that would resist pH changes.

questions.explanation: A basic buffer solution is formed by mixing a weak base with a salt of that weak base and a strong acid. The weak base neutralizes added acids, while the salt provides the conjugate acid form necessary to neutralize added bases. Option B is correct because it describes this combination, where the weak base is paired with a salt formed with a strong acid. Options A and D are incorrect because they do not provide the necessary components for a basic buffer. Option C describes the formation of an acidic buffer, which is not what the question asks for.

questions.text: Hydrolysis of a salt yields Ca(OH)2 and HCl. The nature of this hydrolysed salt is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A basic salt would yield a strong base and a weak acid upon hydrolysis.
An acidic salt yields a strong acid and a weak base upon hydrolysis. Since Ca(OH)2 is a strong base and HCl is a strong acid, the salt is neutral hence this option is incorrect.
Strong base: Ca(OH)₂Strong acid: HClSuch salts generally do not produce an acidic or basic solution; hence they are neutral.
An amphoteric substance can act as both an acid and a base, but this does not apply to the salt in question.

questions.explanation: The hydrolysis of the salt produces Ca(OH)2, which is a strong base, and HCl, which is a strong acid. The presence of a strong acid and base indicates that the salt is neutral in nature. Basic salts would produce a strong base and a weak acid . Amphoteric compounds can act as both acids and bases, but this does not describe the behavior of the salt in this reaction.

questions.text: If 100 molecules of acetic acid are ionised into acetate and hydronium ions out of 1000 molecules, then the number of ions produced by the dissociation of 100 moles of acetic acid will be
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This result corresponds to the number of ions produced if the ionization proportion is applied to one mole of acetic acid.
This option represents the correct number of ions when considering the ionization proportion and Avogadro's number for 100 moles.
This is incorrect because it only accounts for one mole, not 100 moles.
This option is incorrect as it overestimates the number of ions produced for 100 moles.

questions.explanation: To solve this problem, we need to understand that 100 molecules of acetic acid ionize out of 1000, which means 10% of acetic acid molecules ionize. When 100 moles of acetic acid are considered, each mole contains Avogadro's number of molecules, which is approximately 6.02 x 10^23. Therefore, 10% of 100 moles of acetic acid will ionize, producing two ions (acetate and hydronium) for each molecule. Hence, the total number of ions produced is 10% of 100 moles x 2 x 6.02 x 10^23 = 6.02 x 10^24 ions. Therefore, the correct answer is Option B. Option A and C are incorrect as they only consider 1 mole of acetic acid, and Option D is incorrect as it grossly overestimates the number of ions.

questions.text: A basic buffer is prepared by mixing a weak base and a salt of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Weak acid with a strong acid.
options.explanation: This combination would lead to a strong acidic solution, not a buffer.

options.text: Weak base with a strong acid.
options.explanation: Mixing a weak base with the salt of its conjugate acid, formed from a strong acid, creates a basic buffer solution.

options.text: Weak acid with a strong base.
options.explanation: This combination typically forms an acidic buffer, not a basic one.

options.text: Weak base with a strong base.
options.explanation: This does not create a buffer as there is no conjugate acid present to neutralize added acids.

questions.explanation: A basic buffer solution is formed by combining a weak base with its conjugate acid. This can be achieved by mixing a weak base with a salt derived from the reaction of the base with a strong acid. For example, ammonia (NH₃) mixed with ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl) forms a basic buffer. The strong acid in the salt ensures the presence of the conjugate acid necessary for buffering. The other options do not meet the criteria for forming a basic buffer. A weak acid with a strong acid simply results in an acidic solution. A weak acid with a strong base typically forms an acidic buffer, while combining bases does not lead to a buffer solution.

questions.text: If the pH of a solution is 10, then the hydroxide ion concentration in the solution will be
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests a direct correlation between pH and hydroxide ion concentration without accounting for pOH.
This option mistakenly applies the relationship between pH and pOH, resulting in an incorrect hydroxide ion concentration.
This is the correct answer because a pH of 10 corresponds to a pOH of 4, leading to a hydroxide ion concentration of 10-4 M.
This option miscalculates the relationship between pH and pOH, resulting in an incorrect hydroxide ion concentration.

questions.explanation: The pH of a solution indicates the concentration of hydrogen ions, while the pOH indicates the concentration of hydroxide ions. The equation pH + pOH = 14 helps to find the complementary value. For a solution with a pH of 10, the pOH is 4 (since 10 + 4 = 14). Therefore, the hydroxide ion concentration is given by [OH-] = 10-pOH = 10-4 M. Option C is correct because it accurately reflects this calculation. Options A and B incorrectly interpret the relationship between pH, pOH, and hydroxide ion concentration, while option D inverts the correct concentration.

questions.text: In the given reaction, the reactant H2O can be classified as a/ an
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A Lewis acid is an electron pair acceptor. Consider if H2O can accept electron pairs.
A Lewis base is an electron pair donor. Determine if H2O can donate electron pairs.
Consider if H2O plays a role in electron pair donation or acceptance, beyond being neutral.
Amphoteric substances can act as both an acid and a base, but focus on H2O's role in this specific reaction.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Lewis base. In the context of Lewis theory, a Lewis base is defined as a substance that can donate a pair of electrons. Water (H2O) has a pair of non-bonding electrons on the oxygen atom, which allows it to donate an electron pair to form a bond, thus acting as a Lewis base.Option A is incorrect because water is not typically an electron pair acceptor, which is characteristic of Lewis acids.Option C is incorrect because while water is chemically neutral, its role in this context is defined by its ability to donate electron pairs.Option D is incorrect because although water is amphoteric, the question focuses on its role as a Lewis base in this specific reaction.

questions.text: When methanol reacts with water, it acts as a Brønsted-Lowry acid with a position lying very far to the left.Which of the following options CORRECTLY depicts the strength of aci in the given reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Methanol is a strong acid and its conjugate base is also weak as it does not readily accept protons.
If the acid were stronger, the reaction would favor the formation of its conjugate base, which contradicts the scenario.
This is correct.CH3OH is the weaker acid (Acid: Weak), its conjugate base, CH3O must be the stronger base (Conjugate base: Stronger).
Both being stronger is inconsistent with the equilibrium position lying far to the left, indicating weak acid and base.

questions.explanation: Methanol acts as a Brønsted-Lowry acid in its reaction with water, but it is a very weak acid. In the Brønsted-Lowry theory, a weak acid has a weak conjugate base, meaning its conjugate base has little tendency to reaccept a proton. Therefore, the equilibrium strongly favors the left side (reactants), which means both the acid and its conjugate base are weak. Thus, Option Cis correct. Options A,B and D incorrectly describe the relative strengths of the acid and conjugate base given the equilibrium position of the reaction.

questions.text: A solution shows a reading of 7.21 on a pH meter. This indicates that the solution is a
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A strong base typically has a pH much higher than 7.
A strong acid usually has a pH well below 7.
A weak base has a pH slightly above 7, which aligns with the reading of 7.21.
A weak acid has a pH slightly below 7, which does not match the reading of 7.21.

questions.explanation: The pH scale ranges from 0 to 14, with 7 being neutral. A pH less than 7 indicates an acidic solution, while a pH greater than 7 indicates a basic solution. A pH of 7.21 is slightly above neutral, suggesting a weakly basic solution. Therefore, the correct answer is that the solution is a weak base. A strong base would have a pH significantly higher than 7, and a strong acid would have a pH much lower than 7. A weak acid would show a pH slightly below 7.

questions.text: The relationship between the acid dissociation constant (Ka) and the base dissociation constant (Kb) is represented as
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests a summation relationship, which is incorrect for dissociation constants.
This option suggests a multiplication relationship between pK values, which is incorrect.
This option correctly represents the multiplicative relationship between Kw, Ka, and Kb.
This option suggests a subtraction relationship between pK values. While pKw = pKa + pKb describes the correct relationship, this option is presented incorrectly.

questions.explanation: The correct relationship is Kw = Ka × Kb, where Kw is the ion-product constant of water. This reflects the equilibrium condition of water, where the product of the concentrations of H3O+ and OH− equals Kw. Option C correctly describes this multiplicative relationship. Option A is incorrect as it suggests addition, which is not how these constants relate. Option B is incorrect because pKw = pKa + pKb, not multiplied. Option D is incorrect because it presents the relationship between pK values incorrectly; the correct relationship is an addition, not subtraction.

questions.text: If a concentrated sulphuric acid solution is spilled on a person’s skin, then the immediate strategy that can be used to neutralise this acid is to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sodium hydroxide is a strong base and can cause further damage to the skin when reacting with sulphuric acid.

options.text: Apply cold cream on the affected part of skin
options.explanation: Cold cream does not neutralize acids; it is used for skincare and may not prevent further damage from the acid.

options.text: Wash the affected area with water and apply toothpaste
options.explanation: Toothpaste is not effective at neutralizing strong acids and is not recommended for chemical burns.

options.text: Wash the affected area with water and apply a baking soda solution
options.explanation: Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) is a mild base that can help neutralize the acid after thoroughly rinsing with water.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is to wash the affected area with water and apply baking soda solution. Initially, flushing with water dilutes and removes much of the acid, minimizing damage. Baking soda, being a mild base, further helps by neutralizing any residual acid. Sodium hydroxide, although a base, is too strong and can cause additional burns. Cold cream does not address the chemical nature of the acid, and toothpaste is not designed for such neutralization.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT about a neutralisation reaction between a strong acid and a strong base?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Neutralisation reactions usually release heat, making them exothermic.

options.text: 13.7 Kcal heat is used for bond breaking
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Heat is released during the reaction, not used for bond breaking.

options.text: Both the solutions are completely miscible
options.explanation: Strong acids and bases in aqueous solutions are fully miscible.

options.text: Strong acids and bases are completely ionised.

The pOH is calculated using the formula pOH = -log[OH⁻] = -log(0.0125), which equals approximately 1.90. The pH is then found using the relation pH + pOH = 14, giving pH = 14 - 1.90 = 12.10. questions.explanation: To determine the pH of a buffer solution containing C6H5COONa and its conjugate acid, the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is applied: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]). Here, [A-] is the concentration of the salt (C6H5COONa), and [HA] is the concentration of the acid (C6H5COOH). Given the concentration of C6H5COONa is 0.04 M, you would also need the concentration of C6H5COOH. However, if the concentration of the acid is assumed to be similar or you are calculating the pH when they are equimolar, the pH will be close to the pKa value. The pKa is calculated from the given Ka value: pKa = -log(6.4 x 10^-5) ≈ 4.19. However, if the concentration of the base form is higher than the acid form, the pH will be slightly lower than the pKa, leading to the correct answer of 3.89. Other options are incorrect due to errors in applying the equation or assumptions about concentrations.

questions.text: The acid that has the LEAST harmful effect on animals and plants is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hydrochloric acid is present in the stomachs of animals, aiding in digestion but can be harmful in concentrated forms.
Nitric acid is highly corrosive and can cause significant damage to living tissues and the environment.
Carbonic acid is a weak acid that naturally occurs in rainwater and is involved in the carbon cycle, having minimal harmful effects on organisms.
Sulfuric acid is very strong and corrosive, posing substantial risks to living organisms and the environment.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid). Carbonic acid is a weak acid formed when carbon dioxide dissolves in water. It is a natural component of the carbon cycle and occurs in rainwater. Its low acidity makes it the least harmful to animals and plants among the options provided. In contrast, HCl (hydrochloric acid), while naturally present in gastric acid, is harmful in higher concentrations. HNO₃ (nitric acid) and H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) are both strong acids, with significant corrosive properties that can severely damage living tissues and ecosystems.
